pattern: A fixed-size sliding window tracks character frequency differences between the current window and pattern; incrementally updating the difference count detects anagram windows in linear time.
hints: An anagram of p is any permutation of its characters, so the question reduces to: at how many starting indices does a substring of length len(p) have the same character frequencies as p?
Maintain a frequency difference array for the window vs pattern; when all 26 counts are zero the window is an anagram — slide the window by adding the new right character and removing the leftmost.

Initialize by counting the first len(p) characters, then slide from index len(p) onward: add s[r], subtract p's count; remove s[r-len(p)], add back p's count; record the start when the window matches.

edge_inputs.description: p longer than s — no anagram possible
p same length as s, entire string is or isn't anagram
single character string and pattern match
single character pattern, find all occurrences in s
no anagram windows — characters don't overlap with p
p has duplicate characters, window must match exact counts

stress.description: long string and pattern over small alphabet
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, size)
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('abcd') for _ in range(n))
    plen = rng.randrange(1, min(n+1, 20))
    p = ''.join(rng.choice('abcd') for _ in range(plen))
    return [s, p]
